fMHiftr 11=11
At the appearance of sage Agastya (Canopus) the waters that
are polluted (in the rainy season) by their contact with mud become
clear once again spontaneously, just as the minds (of men) that are
corrupted by association with the wicked become pure at the sight
of the virtuous.
[Sanskrit poets are fond of comparing the human mind to
water. An agitated mind is likewise compared to agitated or turbid
water.
Cf. XSVV IV. 21.
f & feqrt *r*T: 11
The metre is
Next the poet describes autumn in 4 verses.]
The autumn which nurtures a row of cackling swans flanked
on both sides by ruddy geese, shines like a smiling damsel whose
front teeth are smeared with the red tinge of betel leaves.
